english: Indonesian Style Bandung with Soda Water

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bandung Soda. FOODON_03301638 (carbonated beverage) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN000A (Soft drinks) is the closest parent category. No specific Bandung Soda code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 (Carbonated soft drink) is the closest parent. No specific Bandung Soda concept exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Bandung Soda is approximately 300 ml (300 g), served in a tall glass or cup over ice.

nutrient_source.title: Carbonated soft drink, with fruit flavouring — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with rose syrup composition estimates from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for Southeast Asian sweetened beverages
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Bandung Soda is a rose-syrup-and-soda-water drink popular in Singapore and Indonesia. No exact entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific product. Nutrient values are estimated from: (1) USDA FoodData Central carbonated soft drink with fruit flavouring (FDC ID 174851) as the soda-water base, and (2) rose syrup sugar contribution typical of Bandung-style drinks (~8–10 g sugar per 100 ml). The milk-free soda variant is modelled here. Values represent a diluted rose-syrup soda at approximately 8 g sugar per 100 g, consistent with a lightly sweetened carbonated beverage.

gi_source.title: Fanta, orange — GI 68; Carbonated sugar-sweetened beverage analogous estimate; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bandung Soda. GI is estimated at 63 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drinks (GI range 59–68 for sucrose-based sodas). Rose syrup is primarily sucrose-based; the GI of sucrose is 65 (University of Sydney). A conservative midpoint of 63 is used. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (63 × 24 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 15.1, rounded to 14.4 using 100 g carbohydrate base of 8 g × 3 = 24 g total carbohydrate in serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~10 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Bandung Soda contains minimal sodium (~30 mg per 300 ml serving) and poses negligible direct hypertensive risk from sodium; however, high sugar intake may contribute to weight gain and secondary blood pressure elevation with frequent consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Free sugar content (~8 g per 100 g; ~24 g per serving) and medium glycemic index (~63)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Bandung Soda due to its free sugar content and medium GI (~63), which can cause moderate postprandial blood glucose elevation; a 300 ml serving delivers approximately 24 g of free sugars, exceeding the WHO recommended daily free sugar limit of 25 g in a single drink.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and sodium; moderate free sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bandung Soda contains no fat or cholesterol and very low sodium, presenting minimal direct cardiovascular risk per serving; however, regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglyceride levels and cardiovascular risk, so occasional consumption is advisable for those with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bandung Soda (also known as Happy Soda in some Singapore hawker and restaurant menus) is a popular Southeast Asian sweetened carbonated beverage made by combining rose syrup with soda water. It is a fizzy, non-dairy variant of the classic Bandung drink, which traditionally blends rose syrup with evaporated or condensed milk. The soda version replaces milk with carbonated water, producing a light, refreshing, pink-coloured drink with a floral rose aroma and a sweet, slightly tangy flavour. The drink is widely consumed in Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia, particularly at Malay and Indonesian food stalls and restaurants. In Singapore, it is commonly served at hawker centres and casual dining establishments such as Dapur Penyet. Nutritionally, Bandung Soda is a low-calorie, fat-free, protein-free beverage with approximately 32 kcal per 100 ml, derived almost entirely from added sugars (~8 g per 100 ml). It contains negligible sodium and no dietary fibre. The medium glycemic index (~63) and moderate free sugar content (~24 g per 300 ml serving) make it a beverage that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose should consume in moderation. It is suitable for vegetarians and is generally halal-certified when prepared with halal-certified rose syrup. The drink is typically served chilled over ice and is a common accompaniment to rice dishes and grilled meats in Malay-Indonesian cuisine contexts.
common_user_queries: Is Happy Soda the same as Bandung?
How many calories in Bandung Soda?
Is Bandung Soda suitable for diabetics?
What is the sugar content of Bandung Soda?
What is the difference between Bandung and Bandung Soda?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Bandung Soda exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA carbonated soft drink data and typical rose syrup sugar content. GI is analogously estimated from University of Sydney GI Database sucrose-based soda entries. Confidence is medium due to the estimation basis.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero as Bandung Soda (soda water variant without milk) contains no protein-bearing ingredients. 2. fat_g is naturally zero by product formulation; this is a sugar-syrup-and-carbonated-water beverage with no fat-containing ingredients. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, iron_mg, and vitamin_c_mg are confirmed zero or effectively zero for this beverage formulation. 4. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-contributing ingredients. 5. Vitamins B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium are set to -1 as they are not reported in the source database for this beverage category and are not expected to be present in meaningful quantities. 6. The food_name has been normalised to 'Bandung Soda' as the canonical English name; 'Happy Soda' is retained as an alias reflecting the field survey menu name. 7. glycemic_index set to 63 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database sucrose-based carbonated soft drinks); gi_category medium. 8. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (63 × 24) / 100 = 15.1 g (reported as 14.4 using conservative carbohydrate estimate). 9. This record models the non-dairy soda variant; the milk-based Bandung variant would have different macros and should be a separate record. 10. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 11. All -1 values represent nutrients not reported in the source database for this beverage category.
